title: The uncertainty geometry of finite-dimensional position and momentum
authors: Dimpi Thakuria, Shuheng Liu, Giuseppe Vitagliano, Konrad Szymański
abstract: Uncertainty relations are usually stated as bounds on selected combinations of variances, but the full covariance matrix contains substantially richer information about the geometry of quantum state space and about the operational capabilities of quantum systems. Here we characterize the covariance matrices attainable by a finite-dimensional canonical pair of observables related by the discrete Fourier transform, the natural analogue of position and momentum in a finite Hilbert space. We combine analytic arguments with convex-geometric and semidefinite-programming methods based on joint numerical ranges to describe the admissible region through unitary invariants, in particular the trace and determinant of the covariance matrix. This provides a systematic way to identify extremal states, generalizing the notion of minimum-uncertainty states, and to quantify how the discrete uncertainty geometry approaches its continuous counterpart with increasing dimension. We further show that the resulting covariance-matrix characterization has direct consequences for applications: it yields accuracy bounds for multi parameter estimation protocols and separability criteria for finite-dimensional bipartite systems, including discrete analogues of continuous-variable EPR-type witnesses. Our results establish a systematic and versatile platform for connecting uncertainty relations, convex quantum geometry, metrology, and entanglement detection in finite-dimensional systems.
summary: This paper characterizes the allowed region of covariance matrices for discrete position and momentum operators in finite dimensions. It establishes a systematic way to identify minimum-uncertainty states and provides new criteria for entanglement detection and multi-parameter quantum metrology.
main_problem: Characterizing the full uncertainty geometry (the set of achievable covariance matrices) for a finite-dimensional canonical pair of position and momentum observables.
main_result: The authors provide a complete geometric description of the trace-determinant region for these matrices, identifying extremal states and demonstrating that this discrete geometry approaches the continuous-variable limit as dimension increases.
method: The study combines analytic arguments with convex-geometric and semidefinite-programming (SDP) methods based on joint numerical ranges and unitary invariants.
model_or_system: Finite-dimensional Hilbert spaces of dimension d, using a canonical pair of observables related by the discrete Fourier transform (the discrete analogue of position and momentum).
key_observables: Position (Q) and momentum (P) analogues, covariance matrix (Gamma), trace and determinant of the covariance matrix, and the Quantum Fisher Information Matrix.
important_parameters: Dimension (d), trace (tr Gamma), determinant (det Gamma), and temperature (T) for noise models.
main_assumptions: The focus is restricted to the preparation uncertainty viewpoint within finite-dimensional systems with bounded spectra.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows joint numerical ranges for d=4 and d=5; Figure 3 displays the trace-determinant region; Figure 4 shows scaling of estimation bounds; Figure 5 shows the gap between restricted and unrestricted measurement accuracy; Figure 6 shows violation of the separability criterion.
paper_structure: The paper moves from the background of covariance matrices and Fourier-related observables to numerical tools like joint numerical ranges, followed by applications in multi-parameter metrology and bipartite entanglement detection.
why_interesting: It provides a rigorous bridge between discrete-variable and continuous-variable quantum mechanics, offering new tools for entanglement detection and precision sensing in finite-dimensional systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Joint numerical ranges (shaded volumes) of Q, P, and T = Q2 + P2 for dimensions d = 4 and d = 5. Wireframes show level sets of the sum of variances Var(Q) + Var(P), which are paraboloids of revolution in this space. Dots mark the minimum and maximum variance states: for odd d, the minima lie on the ⟨Q⟩= ⟨P⟩= 0 axis, while for even d they form a fourfold-symmetric orbit away from it (two of four are visible in (a)). The maximum always lies on the central axis.

FIG. 2: Joint numerical range of i[Q, P], {Q, P}, Q2 −P2 for states restricted to fixed values of ⟨Q2 + P2⟩, ⟨Q⟩, ⟨P⟩. For each such choice of constraints, points on the boundary farthest from the origin correspond to minimal determinant, while points closest to the origin correspond to maximal determinant.

FIG. 3: Allowed region in the (trΓϱ, det Γϱ)-plane for the finite-dimensional canonical pair (Q, P) for dimension d = 3. Two cases are depicted: mixed quantum states (outer region with thick outline and light red background) and pure states (light blue). The pure state region is strictly contained in the mixed-state region.

FIG. 4: Value of Ad, Ac d as functions of dimension in a log-log scale. The quantity Ad, represented by the red dots, provides a lower bound to the trace of the covariance matrix of the two estimators ˜r = (˜r1, ˜r2) (cf. Eq. (38)), and corresponds to optimization performed without imposing the saturability constraint. In contrast, the quantity Ac d represented by the orange dots, corresponds to optimization with the saturability constraint imposed.

FIG. 5: In plot 1, the quantity AM d is shown as a function of the dimension on a log-log scale. This quantity provides a lower bound on the trace of the covariance matrix associated with the two estimators ˜r = (˜r1, ˜r2) (cf. Eq. (48)) with measurement M = R, and an initial state |ψ0⟩, chosen as the state that attains the minimal QCRB Ad (cf. Eq. (39)). Plot 2 shows the difference, ∆= AM d −Ad as a function of the dimension.
